title: Optimizing the Sensitivity-Noise Trade-off in Non-Hermitian Sensing via Off-Exceptional-Deficiency Operation
abstract: A central challenge in non-Hermitian sensing is that spectral singularities simultaneously amplify both the signal and environmental noise. We address this predicament in a double-chain Hatano-Nelson model featuring unidirectional interlayer coupling. At the exceptional deficiency (ED) limit, the system exhibits a macroscopically degenerate complex spectrum and a pronounced non-Hermitian skin effect (NHSE), yielding a sensitivity that scales exponentially with lattice size $N$ while remaining robust across a six-order-of-magnitude detuning range. By introducing diagonal spatial disorder, we demonstrate that the NHSE is progressively suppressed, whith eigenspace cosine similarity analysis quantifying a well-defined fault-tolerance threshold. To reconcile the sensitivity-noise trade-off, we delineate "At-ED" and "Off-ED" operating regimes. While the At-ED configuration imposes fractional-order noise amplification (SNR $\propto δ^{-1/2}$) that saturates at a suboptimal plateau, migrating to the Off-ED regime eliminates this geometric singularity and restores a linear scaling law (SNR $\propto δ^{-1}$), achieving an SNR enhancement of several orders of magnitude. Crucially, this improvement is achieved while fully preserving the exponential sensitivity scaling, albeit at a slightly reduced absolute sensitivity compared to the strict At-ED limit. Our findings establish the Off-ED framework as a concrete paradigm for next-generation topological sensors that reconcile extreme sensitivity with robust noise immunity.
summary: This paper tackles the fundamental trade-off between extreme sensitivity and noise immunity in non-Hermitian quantum sensors. By proposing an 'Off-ED' operating regime, the authors show how to move away from a mathematically singular point to achieve a vastly superior, linearly scaling Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR). This establishes a robust framework for practical, high-performance topological sensing devices.
main_problem: The central challenge addressed is reconciling the extreme sensitivity of non-Hermitian sensors with the simultaneous amplification of environmental noise (the sensitivity-noise trade-off).
main_result: The authors propose an 'Off-ED' operating regime that eliminates pathological geometric singularities, restoring a linear scaling law for the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) while preserving exponential sensitivity scaling.
method: The analysis involves comparing the performance at the strict Exceptional Deficiency (ED) limit versus operating slightly away from it (Off-ED), using noise matrix analysis and scaling law derivations.
model_or_system: The system is a double-chain Hatano-Nelson model featuring unidirectional interlayer coupling, analyzed using a non-Hermitian Hamiltonian with an off-diagonally asymmetric block structure.
key_observables: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR), spectral splitting ($\Delta E_n$), Eigenspace Cosine Similarity ($C$), and the background bias ($\kappa_2$).
important_parameters: Exceptional Deficiency (ED) limit, Off-ED regime, disorder strength ($\alpha$), and noise magnitude ($\delta$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the highly asymmetric interlayer coupling condition and the ability to model noise via a global noise matrix.
figures_summary: Figures compare SNR scaling ($	ext{SNR} \propto \delta^{-1/2}$ vs. $	ext{SNR} \propto \delta^{-1}$) and show the structural robustness of the system under varying levels of spatial disorder.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the trade-off by first establishing the ED limit's limitations, then introducing the Off-ED framework to restore linear noise scaling, and finally demonstrating this improvement across various noise channels and disorder levels.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, actionable paradigm for designing next-generation topological sensors by offering a method to bypass fundamental noise limitations inherent at idealized singular points.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the double-chain Hatano–Nelson model with asymmetric interlayer coupling. Chain A (blue, up- per) and chain B (red, lower) feature opposite nonreciprocal nearest-neighbor hopping with amplitudes ta ±γa and tb ±γb, respectively. Solid and dashed arrows denote the dominant forward coupling κ1 and reverse coupling κ2, respectively, sat- isfying κ1 ≫κ2.

FIG. 2. Eigenspace coalescence and real-space skin-mode distribution under open boundary conditions. (a), (b) Cosine similarity C of the eigenspace as a function of interlayer coupling strength (|κ1| or |κ2|) and hopping parameter ta, for B →A and A →B coupling configurations, respectively. Insets display C versus κ1,2 along two representative cuts at ta = 1.0, tb = 1.0, γa = 0.5, γb = 0.5 (black line, ED) and ta = 2.0, tb = 1.0, γa = 0.5, γb = 0.25 (red line, off-ED). (c), (d) Real-space spatial density G of all eigenstates as a function of |κ1| for B →A coupling. (e), (f) Corresponding spatial density G as a function of |κ2| for A →B coupling.

FIG. 3. Effects of on-site spatial disorder on eigenstate localization and eigenspace coalescence under open bound- ary conditions (N = 50). (a), (b) Ensemble-averaged real- space probability density of the OBC eigenstates under vary- ing disorder strengths α = 0.0, 0.6, 1.2, 1.8 for the B →A and A →B coupling configurations, respectively. (c), (d) Eigenspace cosine similarity C as a function of the disorder mask fraction p for disorder strengths α = 0.6 and α = 1.8. Each data point (blue dot) represents the ensemble mean over multiple disorder realizations, with the red error bars indicat- ing the corresponding standard deviation.

FIG. 4. Sensing performance of the double-chain Hatano-Nelson system. (a) Sensitivity S as a function of perturbation strength ∆κ2 for various system sizes N. (b) Sensitivity S versus lattice size N at a fixed target signal ∆κ2 = 10−8, comparing the At-ED and Off-ED (κ2 = 10−6) operating regimes. (c) Sensitivity S as a function of background bias κ2 for different system sizes N in the Off-ED configuration. All results are obtained under open boundary conditions.

FIG. 5. Signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) comparison between the At-ED and Off-ED operating regimes. (a) SNR as a function of system size N at a fixed noise level δ = 10−8 for the At- ED and Off-ED (κ2 = 10−6) configurations, where dashed lines indicate the respective ensemble means. (b) SNR as a function of the target signal strength ∆κ2 at δ = 10−8. (c) SNR as a function of noise level δ at the strict ED point, with the dashed line showing the theoretical δ−1/2 scaling. (d) Corresponding SNR versus δ in the Off-ED regime, with the solid line showing the theoretical δ−1 scaling.
